description: CrackStation's password cracking tools and dictionaries. Includes massive wordlists and lookup tables for offline password hash cracking using precomputed tables.
how_it_works: The CrackStation approach uses precomputed hash lookup tables rather than brute-force computation. The included wordlists contain billions of real-world passwords from data breaches, sorted by frequency. When cracking a hash, the tool looks it up in the precomputed table for instant results, rather than computing hashes on the fly. This is dramatically faster than traditional brute-force for common passwords.
usage_scenarios: Offline password hash cracking. Testing password strength against real-world breach data. Demonstrating the weakness of common passwords. Security awareness training.
strengths: Precomputed lookup tables for instant cracking
Real breach data - passwords people actually use
weaknesses: Huge download size (15GB+ for full set)
Cannot crack salted hashes with lookup tables
Ethical concerns with breach data
Only covers common passwords in the dataset
comparison: RockYou wordlist is smaller but most commonly used. SecLists provides curated wordlists for different scenarios. HaveIBeenPwned offers password hash checking without distributing plaintext. CrackStation's advantage is its massive size.

name: dirstalk
description: Dirstalk is a multi-threaded directory discovery tool inspired by DirBuster, written in Go. It uses sitemap-based scanning and supports custom dictionary files for discovering hidden paths on web servers.
how_it_works: Dirstalk uses Go's concurrency model for fast multi-threaded scanning. It can use standard wordlists or parse existing sitemap.xml files to build targeted dictionaries. The tool supports HTTP/HTTPS, configurable threads, request throttling, and result filtering by status code. It maintains a persistent connection pool for efficiency and can resume interrupted scans.
usage_scenarios: Directory discovery with sitemap-informed wordlists. Fast multi-threaded content discovery. Scanning with custom dictionaries for targeted testing.
weaknesses: Less well-known than ffuf or Gobuster
Smaller community and fewer features
No recursive scanning mode
Limited output format options
comparison: ffuf and Gobuster are more popular and feature-rich. Feroxbuster provides recursive scanning. DirBuster (Java) is the original but is slow and deprecated. Dirstalk's sitemap feature is unique.

name: dontgo403
description: Dontgo403 is a tool that attempts to bypass 403 (Forbidden) pages using various HTTP request manipulation techniques. It tries multiple bypass methods to access restricted resources.
how_it_works: Dontgo403 sends multiple variations of the original request with modifications designed to bypass access controls: URL path manipulation (adding /, %2f, /.), HTTP method changes (GET to POST, PUT, OPTIONS, HEAD), header modifications (X-Forwarded-For, X-Original-URL, X-Rewrite-URL), and URL encoding variations. For each variation, it checks if the response changes from 403 to 200 or another success code, indicating the access control was bypassed.
usage_scenarios: Bypassing 403 restrictions on web application paths. Testing access control implementations. Finding hidden admin panels and restricted directories. Demonstrating access control misconfigurations.
strengths: Automated 403 bypass testing with multiple techniques
Proxy support for routing through tools
weaknesses: Many bypass attempts may not work on properly configured servers
Can trigger rate limiting or WAF blocks
Limited to common bypass techniques
No exploitation beyond access bypass
comparison: Burp Suite's Autorize extension tests authorization bypass. Manual testing with curl can achieve the same results. This tool automates the common bypass techniques for efficiency.

name: fuxploider
description: Fuxploider is an automated file upload vulnerability scanner and exploitation tool. It detects and exploits file upload forms by testing various file types and extensions to find bypass opportunities.
how_it_works: Fuxploider identifies file upload forms on web pages, then systematically tests them with different file types and extensions. It tries techniques like double extensions (shell.php.jpg), null byte injection (shell.php%00.jpg), content-type manipulation (changing MIME type), and magic byte injection (adding image file headers to PHP files). When an upload succeeds and is accessible, it confirms the vulnerability. The tool uses a headless browser for JavaScript-heavy upload forms.
usage_scenarios: Testing file upload functionality for security vulnerabilities. Bypassing file type restrictions to upload web shells. Validating upload security controls. Demonstrating the impact of insecure file upload handling.
strengths: Automated file upload testing with bypass techniques
weaknesses: Python dependencies can be complex
Not all bypass techniques work on modern frameworks
Headless browser can be slow
May trigger server-side security controls
comparison: Manual Burp Suite testing provides more control. Weevely generates uploadable PHP shells. Fuxploider automates the discovery and bypass testing process.

name: gobuster-dir
description: Gobuster directory mode specifically targets web directory and file discovery. This is the directory-focused variant of the Gobuster tool with optimizations for web content enumeration.
how_it_works: Same as Gobuster but focused on directory enumeration with optimizations for web-specific patterns. It sends concurrent HTTP requests for each wordlist entry appended to the target URL, supporting custom extensions, status code filtering, and pattern-based discovery.
usage_scenarios: Directory and file enumeration on web servers. Finding hidden content and backup files. Content discovery during web application testing.
weaknesses: Same as Gobuster core weaknesses
comparison: Same as Gobuster main entry.

name: nmap
description: Nmap (Network Mapper) is the undisputed king of network scanning. It's used for network discovery, security auditing, and vulnerability detection, supporting a vast array of scan techniques, OS detection, service enumeration, and NSE scripting.
how_it_works: Nmap sends raw IP packets to target hosts and analyzes the responses. It supports multiple scan techniques: SYN scan (half-open, stealthy), TCP connect (full handshake), UDP scan, TCP FIN/Xmas/Null scans (bypassing some firewalls), and ACK scan (mapping firewall rules). OS fingerprinting uses TCP/IP stack behavior analysis with over 1,000 signatures. Service detection probes open ports with service-specific payloads. The Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE) extends functionality with 600+ scripts for vulnerability detection, exploitation, and network discovery.
usage_scenarios: Network reconnaissance and host discovery. Service enumeration and version detection. OS fingerprinting for target identification. Vulnerability scanning with NSE scripts. Firewall rule mapping and evasion testing.
strengths: Supports every scan technique and protocol
weaknesses: Can be slow scanning large networks or all ports
SYN scans require root privileges
Some firewalls/IDS detect and block Nmap scans
Stealth scanning is increasingly ineffective against modern defenses
comparison: Masscan is faster for port discovery but lacks service detection. RustScan provides faster port discovery with Nmap integration. Angry IP Scanner offers a GUI alternative. Nmap remains the most feature-rich and versatile network scanner.

name: masscan
description: Masscan is the fastest Internet-scale port scanner, capable of scanning the entire Internet in under six minutes. It achieves this through custom TCP/IP stack implementation and asynchronous transmission.
how_it_works: Masscan uses a custom TCP/IP stack that operates independently of the host operating system's network stack. It transmits SYN packets at extremely high rates (up to 10 million packets per second) using asynchronous I/O. Unlike Nmap which processes one target at a time, Masscan shuffles all target/port combinations and processes them in random order, which distributes the scanning load across targets. When a SYN-ACK is received, it completes the handshake to confirm the port is open, then tears down the connection with RST.
usage_scenarios: Internet-scale port scanning for large-scale reconnaissance. Rapid scanning of large CIDR ranges. Pre-Nmap scanning to identify open ports for detailed enumeration. Bug bounty scanning across massive attack surfaces.
strengths: Custom TCP/IP stack for maximum performance
Randomized scanning order to distribute load
weaknesses: No service detection - only identifies open ports
Can overwhelm targets and network infrastructure
Requires careful rate tuning to avoid packet loss
Less accurate than Nmap due to high-speed packet handling
comparison: Nmap is slower but provides comprehensive service detection. RustScan is faster than Nmap with Nmap integration. ZMap is similar for Internet-scale research scanning. Masscan is the fastest dedicated port scanner.

name: rustscan
description: RustScan is a modern port scanner that uses Rust's async I/O to achieve scanning speeds 10x faster than Nmap. It can scan all 65,535 ports in under 5 seconds, then pipes results to Nmap for detailed service enumeration.
how_it_works: RustScan uses Rust's asynchronous networking to send SYN packets at extremely high rates. It divides the port range into batches and processes them concurrently, utilizing all available CPU cores. Unlike Nmap's sequential scanning, RustScan can probe thousands of ports simultaneously. Once open ports are identified, it can automatically invoke Nmap with service-specific scripts against only the open ports, combining RustScan's speed with Nmap's deep enumeration capabilities.
usage_scenarios: Rapid port discovery during time-sensitive engagements. Pre-Nmap scanning to quickly identify open ports. Large-scale network reconnaissance. Initial footprinting when speed is critical.
strengths: 10x faster than Nmap for port discovery
Seamless Nmap integration for deep scanning
Supports CIDR ranges and target lists
weaknesses: Only does port scanning - no service detection on its own
Can overwhelm firewalls and trigger IDS
Requires ulimit adjustment for full speed
Less stealthy than Nmap's slower scan modes
comparison: Masscan is similarly fast but less accurate and doesn't integrate with Nmap. Nmap is slower but provides comprehensive service detection. RustScan's advantage is its speed combined with automatic Nmap integration.

name: openvas
description: OpenVAS (Open Vulnerability Assessment Scanner) is a full-featured vulnerability scanner that performs comprehensive security auditing of network infrastructure, web applications, and systems. It's the open-source fork of Nessus.
how_it_works: OpenVAS uses a client-server architecture with the scanner daemon (openvassd) performing the actual scanning and the manager (openvasmd) handling scan configuration and results. It maintains a database of over 60,000 Network Vulnerability Tests (NVTs) that are continuously updated. Each NVT is a NASL (Nessus Attack Scripting Language) script that tests for a specific vulnerability. Scans are performed by executing NVTs against targets, with results categorized by severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low, Log).
usage_scenarios: Comprehensive vulnerability assessment of enterprise networks. Compliance scanning for PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and other standards. Regular security auditing of infrastructure. Vulnerability management programs.
weaknesses: Complex installation and setup process
Resource-intensive - requires significant RAM and disk
Scans can take hours for large networks
False positives require manual triage
Web UI can be slow with large result sets
comparison: Nessus is the commercial original with better support. Qualys provides cloud-based scanning as a service. Nexpose (Rapid7) offers similar commercial functionality. OpenVAS is the most capable free vulnerability scanner.

name: nessus
description: Nessus is one of the most widely deployed vulnerability scanners in the world, developed by Tenable. It provides comprehensive vulnerability assessment, configuration auditing, and compliance checking with an extensive plugin database.
how_it_works: Nessus uses a plugin architecture where each plugin tests for a specific vulnerability or misconfiguration. Plugins are written in NASL and updated daily. The scanner performs host discovery, port scanning, service identification, and then runs applicable plugins against discovered services. Results are categorized by severity with detailed remediation guidance. Nessus supports authenticated scanning (using provided credentials) for deeper internal vulnerability assessment.
usage_scenarios: Enterprise vulnerability management. Compliance auditing for regulatory requirements. Pre-deployment security assessment. Continuous vulnerability monitoring.
weaknesses: Commercial product - requires paid license for full features
Resource-intensive scanning engine
Can generate high volumes of network traffic
False positives require manual triage
comparison: OpenVAS is the free alternative with similar architecture. Qualys provides cloud-based scanning. Nexpose offers competitive commercial scanning. Nessus is the industry standard for commercial vulnerability scanning.

name: zgrab2
description: ZGrab2 is a fast, modular Internet-scale application-layer scanner. It's the successor to ZGrab and works alongside ZMap for large-scale service fingerprinting and banner grabbing.
how_it_works: ZGrab2 takes IP:port pairs (typically from ZMap output) and performs application-layer handshakes to collect detailed service banners and certificates. It supports multiple modules (HTTP, HTTPS, SSH, TLS, MySQL, PostgreSQL, Redis, etc.) each implementing protocol-specific probing. Results are output as structured JSON for analysis. The tool uses Go's concurrency model for high-throughput scanning and supports rate limiting to control scan speed.
usage_scenarios: Internet-scale service fingerprinting. TLS certificate analysis across large networks. Banner grabbing for service identification. Large-scale security research and measurement studies.
strengths: Integration with ZMap for full pipeline
weaknesses: Requires ZMap or similar for port discovery
Go binary - not easily extensible
Limited active vulnerability testing
No GUI or visualization
comparison: Nmap provides deeper scanning but is much slower. Masscan only does port scanning. ZGrab2 fills the gap for fast application-layer fingerprinting at scale.

name: amass
description: Amass is the most comprehensive subdomain enumeration tool available. It performs DNS enumeration, scraping, and reverse DNS lookups using over 60 data sources including APIs, web archives, and certificate transparency logs.
how_it_works: Amass operates in multiple phases: DNS enumeration (brute-force subdomains with recursive resolution), scraping (querying search engines, DNS aggregators, and web archives), reverse DNS (finding domains associated with IP ranges), and certificate transparency (parsing CT logs for domain names in certificates). It uses over 60 API sources including Shodan, VirusTotal, PassiveTotal, and more. Results are graphed to show domain relationships, and the tool supports active verification of discovered subdomains.
usage_scenarios: Comprehensive subdomain enumeration for attack surface mapping. Passive reconnaissance without alerting the target. Bug bounty subdomain discovery. Asset inventory and shadow IT discovery.
strengths: Both passive and active enumeration modes
weaknesses: API rate limits on free tiers of data sources
Can be slow with all sources enabled
Requires API keys for full functionality
Large result sets require filtering
comparison: Sublist3r is simpler but less comprehensive. Knockpy focuses on subdomain resolution. Subfinder is faster for passive enumeration. Amass provides the deepest enumeration available.

name: subfinder
description: Subfinder is a fast subdomain discovery tool focused on passive enumeration. It queries multiple data sources simultaneously to find subdomains without sending any requests to the target.
how_it_works: Subfinder uses a purely passive approach, querying external data sources (DNS aggregators, search engines, certificate transparency logs, web archives) for subdomain information. It never sends requests directly to the target domain, making it completely stealthy. The tool uses Go's concurrency to query all configured sources simultaneously, then deduplicates and resolves discovered subdomains. It supports API keys for enhanced results from premium sources.
usage_scenarios: Stealthy subdomain enumeration. Fast passive reconnaissance. Bug bounty subdomain discovery. Asset inventory without alerting target.
strengths: Completely stealthy - no direct target contact
weaknesses: Passive only - misses subdomains not in data sources
Results depend on data source coverage
Requires API keys for best results
Cannot verify if subdomains are currently active
comparison: Amass is more comprehensive with active enumeration. Sublist3r is simpler but slower. Knockpy includes DNS resolution. Subfinder is the fastest passive-only subdomain finder.

name: maltego
description: Maltego is an open-source intelligence (OSINT) and graphical link analysis tool that visualizes relationships between entities like people, organizations, domains, and infrastructure. It's essential for complex reconnaissance.
how_it_works: Maltego uses transforms (plugins) that query data sources and return related entities. Starting from a single entity (domain, person, phone number), transforms discover connected entities: DNS records, WHOIS data, social media profiles, email addresses, document metadata, and infrastructure relationships. Results are displayed as an interactive graph where nodes are entities and edges are relationships. Community edition includes basic transforms, while commercial versions add premium data sources.
usage_scenarios: OSINT investigation and link analysis. Mapping organizational structure and infrastructure. Social engineering reconnaissance. Visualizing complex entity relationships.
commands: # Use transforms to expand entities in the graph
# Export graph as image or report
strengths: Visual graph-based analysis for relationship mapping
Hundreds of transforms for diverse data sources
weaknesses: Community edition has limited transforms
Java-based - resource intensive
Learning curve for effective transform selection
Commercial version is expensive
comparison: SpiderFoot provides automated OSINT with similar data sources. Recon-ng offers a framework-based approach. TheHarvester focuses on email and subdomain discovery. Maltego's advantage is visual relationship mapping.

name: spiderfoot
description: SpiderFoot is an automated OSINT collection tool that gathers intelligence from over 200 data sources. It automates the process of collecting and correlating open-source intelligence about IP addresses, domains, email addresses, and more.
how_it_works: SpiderFoot takes a target (IP, domain, email, username, phone number) and automatically queries over 200 OSINT data sources. It collects DNS records, WHOIS data, breach data, social media profiles, web content, and infrastructure information. Results are correlated to find relationships between data points. The tool includes a web UI for interactive analysis and supports automated scanning with email notifications. It can also integrate with other tools like Shodan and HaveIBeenPwned.
usage_scenarios: Automated OSINT collection for penetration test reconnaissance. Email and credential breach checking. Infrastructure mapping and relationship discovery. Continuous monitoring of target entities.
weaknesses: Can generate overwhelming amounts of data
Many data sources require API keys
Python dependencies can be complex
Some data sources are rate-limited
comparison: Maltego provides visual graph analysis but requires more manual work. Recon-ng is a framework for custom OSINT workflows. TheHarvester is simpler for specific data types. SpiderFoot automates the broadest range of OSINT collection.

name: recon-ng
description: Recon-ng is a full-featured web reconnaissance framework with a modular interface similar to Metasploit. It provides a structured environment for performing open-source intelligence gathering with modules for various data sources.
how_it_works: Recon-ng uses a module-based architecture where each module performs a specific reconnaissance task. Modules are organized by function: discovery (finding hosts, contacts, credentials), enumeration (DNS, ports, paths), exploitation (using discovered information), and reporting (generating output). The framework manages a database of discovered information that modules can read from and write to, enabling data sharing between modules. It supports API key management for premium data sources.
usage_scenarios: Structured OSINT gathering with module-based workflows. Automated reconnaissance pipeline creation. Subdomain and contact enumeration. API-integrated intelligence collection.
strengths: Metasploit-like interface familiar to penetration testers
API key management for premium sources
weaknesses: Learning curve for module selection and chaining
Some modules are outdated or broken
Less automated than SpiderFoot
Database can become unwieldy with large result sets
comparison: SpiderFoot provides more automated collection. Maltego offers visual analysis. Amass is deeper for subdomain enumeration specifically. Recon-ng provides a structured framework for custom workflows.

name: theharvester
description: TheHarvester is a tool for gathering emails, subdomains, hosts, employee names, and URLs from public sources. It's designed for the early stages of penetration testing when broad OSINT gathering is needed.
how_it_works: TheHarvester queries multiple search engines (Google, Bing, DuckDuckGo), social networks (LinkedIn, Twitter), DNS servers, and other public data sources for information related to a domain. It extracts email addresses, subdomains, hostnames, and employee names from search results and API responses. The tool uses rate limiting and rotating user agents to avoid detection. Results are output in a structured format for use in subsequent testing phases.
usage_scenarios: Email harvesting for phishing campaigns. Subdomain discovery for attack surface mapping. Employee name gathering for social engineering. Quick OSINT overview of target organization.
strengths: Multiple search engine and social network sources
Email, subdomain, and employee name collection
weaknesses: Search engine rate limiting reduces effectiveness
Some data sources no longer work reliably
Less comprehensive than SpiderFoot or Amass
Results can be noisy with false positives
comparison: SpiderFoot collects from more sources. Amass is deeper for subdomains. Maltego provides relationship visualization. TheHarvester is best for quick, broad OSINT gathering.

name: shodan
description: Shodan is a search engine for Internet-connected devices. Unlike Google which indexes web content, Shodan indexes the services and devices exposed on the Internet, including industrial control systems, databases, and IoT devices.
how_it_works: Shodan continuously crawls the Internet by performing port scans and banner grabbing on all reachable IP addresses. It indexes the banners, service information, and metadata from discovered services. Users can search for specific device types, software versions, geographic locations, and organizations using Shodan's query syntax. The API enables programmatic access for automation and integration with other tools.
usage_scenarios: Discovering exposed services and devices on the Internet. Finding vulnerable software versions. Identifying industrial control systems and IoT devices. Geographic mapping of Internet infrastructure.
weaknesses: Free tier has limited query quota
Data can be outdated for dynamic services
Cannot access services behind NAT or firewalls
Ethical concerns about device exposure
comparison: Censys provides similar Internet-wide scanning with academic backing. ZoomEye is a Chinese alternative. FOFA is another search engine. Shodan remains the most popular and comprehensive.

name: weevely
description: Weevely is a stealth PHP web shell generator and handler that creates obfuscated backdoors and provides a terminal-like interface for post-exploitation through the web shell. It generates polymorphic PHP payloads that evade signature-based detection.
how_it_works: Weevely generates PHP web shells using polymorphic code generation - each generated shell is syntactically unique but functionally identical, making signature-based detection nearly impossible. The generated PHP uses encoding, variable name randomization, and code obfuscation techniques. Communication between the client and the web shell uses HTTP(S) with encrypted payloads, making traffic analysis difficult. The handler provides over 30 post-exploitation modules for file operations, system command execution, database access, network tunneling, and privilege escalation.
usage_scenarios: Maintaining persistent access to compromised web servers. Post-exploitation through web shells. File management on compromised systems. Network pivoting through web shell proxies.
strengths: Stealthy traffic that blends with normal web traffic
weaknesses: PHP-only - no support for other languages
Web shell must be uploaded to target first
Active scans can detect the shell file
Limited compared to full C2 frameworks like Metasploit
comparison: b374k and WSO are simpler PHP shells without encryption. China Chopper is minimal but widely detected. Metasploit's meterpreter provides more powerful post-exploitation but requires different delivery. Weevely's advantage is stealth and PHP-specific obfuscation.
